query: What is the official color of the third oldest surviving university?When ready, output the final answer enclosed in <answer> and </answer> tags. Do not generate any content after the </answer> tag.
query_analysis:
Concise Summary: The query seeks the official color of the third oldest surviving university, requiring identification of the university's ranking by age and its specific institutional colors.

Required Skills:
Historical research to determine university founding dates and ranking, and institutional knowledge or web search to find official branding details.

Relevant Tools:
Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool is ideal for verifying founding dates and university lists; Ground_Google_Search_Tool is necessary to find the specific official color if not listed in general encyclopedic entries.

Additional Considerations:
The definition of 'third oldest surviving' may vary based on criteria (e.g., continuous operation vs. founding date), and official colors might be described in heraldry or modern branding rather than a single named color.

The official color of the University of Cambridge is **Cambridge Blue**. This distinctive color is a soft, muted shade of blue-green, often described as a pale sky blue[3](https://piktochart.com/tips/what-color-is-cambridge-blue), [4](https://formacionpoliticaisc.buenosaires.gob.ar/breaking-review/unveiling-the-iconic-colors-of-cambridge-university-1767648414). The University's official style guide defines Cambridge Blue as Pantone 557 C, with RGB values of R 133, G 176, B 154[5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Blue_(colour)). Its Hex code is #85B09A[5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Blue_(colour)), [6](https://www.designyourway.net/blog/cambridge-university-logo/). Cambridge Blue is a prominent element of the university's identity and is widely used across its communications[7](https://www.cam.ac.uk/brand-resources/guidelines/colour).

While Cambridge Blue is the signature color, maroon is also a significant color that can be found alongside Cambridge Blue in many official capacities, particularly in sports and academic regalia[4](https://formacionpoliticaisc.buenosaires.gob.ar/breaking-review/unveiling-the-iconic-colors-of-cambridge-university-1767648414). The University of Cambridge's brand color palette includes several shades of blue, along with other colors, to provide flexibility in its communications[7](https://www.cam.ac.uk/brand-resources/guidelines/colour)."]}}

result:
The third oldest surviving university is the University of Cambridge, located in Cambridge, England, founded in 1209[1](https://www.designpieces.com/palette/university-of-cambridge-color-palette-hex-and-rgb/), [2](http://www.paulnoll.com/Colors/school-06-Cambridge.html).

The official color of the University of Cambridge is **Cambridge Blue**. This distinctive color is a soft, muted shade of blue-green, often described as a pale sky blue[3](https://piktochart.com/tips/what-color-is-cambridge-blue), [4](https://formacionpoliticaisc.buenosaires.gob.ar/breaking-review/unveiling-the-iconic-colors-of-cambridge-university-1767648414). The University's official style guide defines Cambridge Blue as Pantone 557 C, with RGB values of R 133, G 176, B 154[5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Blue_(colour)). Its Hex code is #85B09A[5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Blue_(colour)), [6](https://www.designyourway.net/blog/cambridge-university-logo/). Cambridge Blue is a prominent element of the university's identity and is widely used across its communications[7](https://www.cam.ac.uk/brand-resources/guidelines/colour).

While Cambridge Blue is the signature color, maroon is also a significant color that can be found alongside Cambridge Blue in many official capacities, particularly in sports and academic regalia[4](https://formacionpoliticaisc.buenosaires.gob.ar/breaking-review/unveiling-the-iconic-colors-of-cambridge-university-1767648414). The University of Cambridge's brand color palette includes several shades of blue, along with other colors, to provide flexibility in its communications[7](https://www.cam.ac.uk/brand-resources/guidelines/colour).
